statusLine: EU fuel security is under strain with 19 of 27 countries holding critical stock levels in at least one fuel type, despite overall averages remaining above the 90-day minimum.
fullAnalysis:
European fuel security faces significant pressure across multiple member states, though aggregate EU stock levels remain above mandatory minimums. While EU-wide averages show 98.7 days for petrol, 97.4 days for diesel, and 91.1 days for jet fuel, these figures mask severe imbalances. Romania presents the most concerning situation with all three fuel types in critical status, including diesel stocks at just 20.2 days. Poland, Spain, Luxembourg, and Germany also show critical levels across all fuel categories.

The data reveals 19 of 27 countries maintain critical stock levels in at least one fuel type, with jet fuel showing particular vulnerability. Only 8 countries - Sweden, Ireland, Slovenia, Estonia, Malta, Finland, Denmark, and Cyprus - maintain safe or watch status across all fuel types. This widespread strain reflects the impact of recent strategic reserve releases following Middle East supply disruptions, compounded by Brent crude's 27% rise since the conflict began.

Fuel pricing shows significant regional variation, with Netherlands consumers paying €2.316 per litre for petrol compared to €1.34 in Malta. The €0.237 per litre premium for diesel over petrol EU-wide indicates continued supply tightness in distillate markets. Current oil prices at $98.34 per barrel, despite a recent $1.05 decline, remain elevated and continue pressuring both stock management and consumer costs across the bloc.
keyPoints: 19 of 27 EU countries maintain critical stock levels in at least one fuel type, with Romania showing the most severe shortages at 20.2 days for diesel
Jet fuel stocks are particularly vulnerable, with 17 countries below safe levels and an EU average of just 91.1 days
Major economies including Germany, France, Italy, and Spain all maintain critical stock levels across multiple fuel categories
Fuel prices vary dramatically across the bloc, with a €0.976 per litre spread between the most expensive (Netherlands) and cheapest (Malta) petrol prices
Brent crude remains elevated at $98.34 per barrel despite recent declines, reflecting ongoing Middle East supply disruption impacts
